section.title: The Gibeonites Avenged
section.sectionRationale: The book's closing appendix opens with a famine that God traces to Saul's broken covenant with the Gibeonites. David seeks atonement, and at the Gibeonites' request seven of Saul's descendants are executed, sparing Mephibosheth for Jonathan's sake. Rizpah's vigil over the bodies moves David to bury Saul's house honorably, and God answers prayer for the land. A closing account of battles against Philistine giants shows David's mighty men finishing the work David began against Goliath. The movement weighs bloodguilt, atonement, and covenant faithfulness.
section.thesis: A famine exposes the unatoned bloodguilt of Saul's broken oath, and atonement is made at terrible cost, while David honors his own covenant with Jonathan and buries Saul's house with dignity, so that broken covenants demand reckoning and kept covenants bring mercy, all pointing to the deeper atonement that God alone can finally provide.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the famine, the Gibeonites' claim, and the execution of Saul's sons with Mephibosheth spared, verses 1 to 9; Rizpah's vigil and the honorable burial of Saul's house, verses 10 to 14; and four battles against the Philistine giants, verses 15 to 22.

units.label: Atonement for Saul's bloodguilt
units.note: the broken oath to the Gibeonites

verses.text: During the reign of David there was a famine for three successive years, and David sought the face of the LORD. And the LORD said, “It is because of the blood shed by Saul and his family, because he killed the Gibeonites.”

At this, David summoned the Gibeonites and spoke to them. (Now the Gibeonites were not Israelites, but a remnant of the Amorites. The Israelites had taken an oath concerning them, but in his zeal for Israel and Judah, Saul had sought to kill them.)
So David asked the Gibeonites, “What shall I do for you? How can I {{kaphar|make amends}} so that you may bless the inheritance of the LORD?”
The Gibeonites said to him, “We need no silver or gold from Saul or his house, nor should you put to death anyone in Israel for us.” “Whatever you ask, I will do for you,” he replied.
And they answered the king, “As for the man who consumed us and plotted against us to exterminate us from existing within any border of Israel,
let seven of his male descendants be delivered to us so that we may hang them before the LORD at Gibeah of Saul, the chosen of the LORD.” “I will give them to you,” said the king.
Now the king spared Mephibosheth son of Jonathan, the son of Saul, because of the {{berit|oath}} before the LORD between David and Jonathan son of Saul.

But the king took Armoni and Mephibosheth, the two sons whom Rizpah daughter of Aiah had borne to Saul, as well as the five sons whom Merab daughter of Saul had borne to Adriel son of Barzillai the Meholathite.

And he delivered them into the hands of the Gibeonites, and they hanged them on the hill before the LORD. So all seven of them fell together; they were put to death in the first days of the harvest, at the beginning of the barley harvest.

And Rizpah the daughter of Aiah took sackcloth and spread it out for herself on a rock. From the beginning of the harvest until the rain from heaven poured down on the bodies, she did not allow the birds of the air to rest on them by day, nor the beasts of the field by night.
When David was told what Saul’s concubine Rizpah, daughter of Aiah, had done,

he went and took the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan from the men of Jabesh-gilead, who had stolen them from the public square of Beth-shan where the Philistines had hung the bodies after they had struck down Saul at Gilboa.
So David had the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan brought from there, and they also gathered the bones of those who had been hanged.

And they buried the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan in Zela in the land of Benjamin, in the tomb of Saul’s father Kish. After they had done everything the king had commanded, God answered their prayers for the land.
Once again the Philistines waged war against Israel, and David and his servants went down and fought against the Philistines. But David became exhausted.
Then Ishbi-benob, a descendant of Rapha, whose bronze spear weighed three hundred shekels and who was bearing a new sword, resolved to kill David.

But Abishai son of Zeruiah came to his aid, struck the Philistine, and killed him. Then David’s men swore to him, “You must never again go out with us to battle, so that the lamp of Israel may not be extinguished.”
Some time later at Gob, there was another battle with the Philistines. At that time Sibbecai the Hushathite killed Saph, a descendant of Rapha.
Once again there was a battle with the Philistines at Gob, and Elhanan son of Jair the Bethlehemite killed the brother of Goliath the Gittite, the shaft of whose spear was like a weaver’s beam.
And there was also a battle at Gath, where there was a man of great stature with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in all. He too was descended from Rapha,
and when he taunted Israel, Jonathan the son of David’s brother Shimei killed him.
So these four descendants of Rapha in Gath fell at the hands of David and his servants.

kaphar.gloss: To atone, cover, make amends, appease. David asks how he can kaphar, make atonement, for the bloodguilt of Saul's broken oath so that the Gibeonites will bless the LORD's inheritance. The word is the great Old Testament term for atonement, covering guilt so that wrath is turned away; here it is made at the cost of life, pointing to the deeper atonement God alone provides in Christ.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding oath sworn before God. Two covenants shape the chapter: the ancient oath Israel swore to the Gibeonites, which Saul broke and which now demands reckoning, and the personal covenant between David and Jonathan, for whose sake David spares Mephibosheth. The chapter turns on the weight of sworn oaths before the LORD, broken to ruin, kept to mercy.

basic.context: This chapter begins the appendix that closes 2 Samuel, gathering episodes from across David's reign that illuminate its themes. A three-year famine drives David to seek the LORD, who reveals its cause: bloodguilt resting on the house of Saul because Saul had broken Israel's ancient sworn oath to spare the Gibeonites and had tried to destroy them. The Gibeonites, when asked how amends can be made, refuse money and ask instead for seven of Saul's male descendants to be executed. David complies but spares Mephibosheth because of his own covenant oath with Jonathan. Seven of Saul's line are handed over and put to death. Then Rizpah, mother of two of the dead, keeps a heartbreaking vigil, guarding the exposed bodies from birds and beasts through the whole harvest season until the rains come. Her devotion moves David to gather the bones of Saul, Jonathan, and the executed men and bury them honorably in the family tomb, and God then answers prayer for the land. The chapter closes with four short accounts of battles in which David and his mighty men kill Philistine giants, descendants of Rapha, finishing the work David had begun against Goliath, though David himself now grows weary and must be protected. The chapter raises hard questions about bloodguilt, atonement, corporate responsibility, and covenant faithfulness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that reflects the ancient understanding of corporate bloodguilt and the seriousness of oaths sworn before God, matters that must be read within their Old Testament covenantal setting rather than transplanted directly into modern individual ethics. The execution of Saul's descendants is deeply troubling to modern readers and is not held up as a timeless model; it is reported as the resolution demanded within that world, where a broken covenant oath had brought bloodguilt on the land. What the chapter teaches enduringly is the weight of covenant faithfulness, the reality of atonement for guilt, and God's response to it, all of which point beyond this costly and imperfect resolution to the perfect atonement in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: David asks how he may kaphar, make atonement, for the bloodguilt Saul incurred, the great Old Testament word for covering guilt so that wrath is turned away. And the chapter turns on the weight of berit, covenant oaths sworn before the LORD: Israel's broken oath to the Gibeonites demands reckoning, while David's kept oath with Jonathan brings mercy to Mephibosheth. Atonement and covenant, the two key words, frame a chapter about guilt that must be covered and oaths that must be honored.
basic.typology: The chapter's search for atonement to lift the curse of bloodguilt from the land, made only at the cost of life, points forward to the true atonement in Christ. Here innocent descendants die for the guilt of another, an imperfect and costly resolution within a fallen world; there the truly innocent One dies for the guilt of all, the perfect atonement that alone finally covers sin and lifts the curse. And David's covenant faithfulness in sparing Mephibosheth for Jonathan's sake pictures the God who keeps covenant and shows mercy for the sake of another.
basic.christ: The chapter's deepest cry is for atonement, kaphar, to cover bloodguilt and turn away wrath from the land. The costly, imperfect resolution here, life given for guilt, points beyond itself to Christ, in whom God provided the true and final atonement, the innocent One dying not for His own guilt but for ours, so that the curse is fully lifted and the wrath turned away forever. And David sparing Mephibosheth for the sake of his covenant with Jonathan pictures the God who, for the sake of His Son and His covenant, shows undeserved mercy to those under judgment.
basic.god: God is the one who reveals the hidden cause of the famine, showing that He takes broken covenant oaths and shed blood with utter seriousness, even generations later. He is a God of justice who will not let bloodguilt lie unatoned, yet also a God who answers prayer for the land once amends are made, and who honors covenant faithfulness. The chapter reveals God's holiness regarding guilt and His responsiveness to atonement, truths that find their resolution in the cross.
basic.crossReferences: Joshua 9 records the oath Israel swore to the Gibeonites that Saul later broke. Numbers 35 teaches that bloodshed pollutes the land and requires atonement. Second Samuel 9 gives David's covenant kindness to Mephibosheth, honored here. And Hebrews 9 declares that without the shedding of blood there is no atonement, fulfilled in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice.
basic.oneStory: A famine exposes unatoned bloodguilt from a broken covenant, and atonement is made at terrible cost, while David honors his own covenant and buries Saul's house with dignity. The chapter's weight of guilt, atonement, and covenant faithfulness points beyond its costly, imperfect resolution to Jesus, in whom God provides the true atonement that finally covers sin, lifts the curse, and keeps covenant mercy forever.

context.literary: This chapter opens the six-part appendix, chapters 21 to 24, arranged thematically rather than chronologically to sum up David's reign. It is paired with chapter 24, another account of a national crisis ended by atonement, framing the appendix. Rizpah's vigil is one of the most poignant scenes in Scripture, and the giant-killing accounts echo and complete David's own victory over Goliath in 1 Samuel 17.
context.historical: The Gibeonites had secured a sworn treaty with Israel in Joshua's day by a ruse, and that oath, though obtained by deception, remained binding before God. Saul's attempt to wipe them out broke it. In the ancient world, unatoned bloodshed was believed to pollute the land and could be answered by executing members of the guilty house. Exposing bodies without burial was a severe dishonor, which is why Rizpah's vigil and David's burial matter so much. The Rephaim were legendary giants; Goliath had been one such Philistine champion.
context.canonical: The chapter's themes of bloodguilt, atonement, and covenant oaths connect to the law of Moses and forward to the New Testament theology of atonement. Rizpah's devotion has been cherished as a picture of faithful love guarding the dead. The defeat of the giants rounds off the Goliath narrative and shows David's legacy carried on by his men, while the note that the 'lamp of Israel' must not be extinguished points to the enduring Davidic line that leads to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that broken covenant oaths and shed blood incur real guilt that God takes seriously and that must be atoned for, and that kept covenants bring mercy, framing David's reign as one marked by the weight of oaths before the LORD and pointing toward the atonement only God can finally provide.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes an ancient resolution to corporate bloodguilt, including the execution of Saul's descendants, within its Old Testament covenantal world; it does not prescribe such practices for readers today, and modern individual ethics must be shaped by the fuller revelation in Christ. What endures is the seriousness of covenant faithfulness, the reality that guilt requires atonement, God's responsiveness once amends are made, and the honoring of the dead and of oaths.

debates.question: How should we understand the execution of Saul's descendants for Saul's sin?

views.label: Corporate bloodguilt resolved within its ancient covenantal setting
views.case: In the Old Testament world, a king's breaking of a national covenant oath brought bloodguilt on the whole royal house and polluted the land, and the injured party, the Gibeonites, had the right to seek redress; the narrative reports this as the resolution that lifted the famine, reflecting the era's understanding of corporate responsibility and the gravity of a sworn oath before the LORD, not a timeless endorsement of executing the innocent for another's crime, which the law itself elsewhere forbids, Deuteronomy 24:16.

views.label: A troubling episode the text records without fully commending
views.case: Others stress that the narrative reports these events soberly and that the request came from the Gibeonites, not from an explicit divine command to execute the seven; on this reading the chapter honestly records a harsh ancient resolution to bloodguilt, and its abiding lesson is the seriousness of covenant-breaking and the need for atonement, while the disturbing means belong to a world awaiting the fuller light of Christ.

debates.bearing: Both agree the passage reflects an ancient world of corporate bloodguilt and covenant oaths far from modern individual ethics, and that it is descriptive rather than prescriptive; its enduring point is the gravity of broken covenants and the necessity of atonement, which drives the reader to the perfect and just atonement in Christ, where the innocent One freely gives Himself for the guilty.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's theological center is the word kaphar, to atone, cover, make amends: David asks the Gibeonites, 'How can I make atonement so that you may bless the inheritance of the LORD?' verse 3. This is the great Old Testament word for covering guilt so that wrath is turned away, and here that covering is sought for the bloodguilt Saul incurred by breaking a sworn oath. The whole episode is governed by the weight of berit, covenant: Israel had sworn a binding oath to spare the Gibeonites, Joshua 9, and Saul's violation of that berit brought guilt on his house and famine on the land, while David's own berit with Jonathan moves him to spare Mephibosheth from the reckoning. So the two words, atonement and covenant, carry the chapter's meaning: oaths sworn before the LORD are not trivial, their breaking incurs real guilt that must be covered, and their keeping brings mercy. Both point beyond this costly, imperfect resolution to the true kaphar God provides in Christ and the new covenant sealed in His blood.

typology.type: Atonement made at the cost of life to lift the curse from the land
typology.fulfillment: Christ, whose death is the true atonement that lifts the curse and turns away wrath, Galatians 3:13 and Hebrews 9:22.

typology.type: Mephibosheth spared for the sake of a covenant with another
typology.fulfillment: Sinners spared and shown mercy for the sake of Christ and the covenant in His blood, Ephesians 1:6-7.

typology.type: The lamp of Israel that must not be extinguished
typology.fulfillment: The enduring Davidic line preserved by God until the coming of Christ, the light of the world, John 8:12 and 1 Kings 11:36.

study.christ: The chapter's deepest cry is for atonement, kaphar, to cover bloodguilt and turn God's wrath away from the land. Its costly, imperfect resolution, life given to cover the guilt of a broken oath, points beyond itself to Christ, in whom God provided the true and final atonement: the innocent One dying not for His own guilt but for ours, becoming a curse for us to redeem us from the curse, Galatians 3:13, so that wrath is turned away forever and without the shedding of His blood there could be no forgiveness, Hebrews 9:22. And David sparing Mephibosheth for the sake of his covenant with Jonathan pictures the God who, for the sake of His beloved Son and the covenant sealed in His blood, shows undeserved mercy to those otherwise under judgment, Ephesians 1:6-7. What the seven could only imperfectly and tragically foreshadow, Christ fulfills perfectly and justly, the guiltless dying willingly so the guilty may live.
study.god: God is the one who reveals the hidden cause of the three-year famine, showing that He takes broken covenant oaths and shed blood with utter seriousness, even when the crime lies generations back and the guilty king is dead. He is a God of justice who will not let bloodguilt lie unatoned upon the land, and yet also a God who answers prayer for the land once amends have been made, and who honors covenant faithfulness by sparing Mephibosheth. The chapter reveals God's holiness regarding guilt, His regard for sworn oaths, and His responsiveness to atonement, truths that find their full and righteous resolution not in the execution of Saul's sons but in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly.

crossReferences.note: The oath Israel swore to spare the Gibeonites, binding before God even though obtained by deception, which Saul later broke.
Bloodshed pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for it except by the blood of the one who shed it, the background to the chapter's bloodguilt.
Christ redeemed us from the curse by becoming a curse for us, the true atonement the chapter's costly resolution foreshadows.
Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness, fulfilled in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice.

study.oneStory: A famine exposes the unatoned bloodguilt of Saul's broken covenant, and atonement is made at terrible, imperfect cost, while David honors his own covenant with Jonathan and buries Saul's dishonored house with dignity, and God answers prayer for the land. The chapter's heavy themes of guilt, atonement, and covenant faithfulness point beyond their harsh ancient resolution to Jesus, in whom God provides the true atonement that finally and justly covers sin, lifts the curse, and keeps covenant mercy forever. The famine-ending sacrifice of Saul's sons is a dark shadow of the world-redeeming sacrifice of David's greater Son.

quiz.q: What does the LORD reveal as the cause of the three-year famine?

options.text: Bloodguilt on Saul's house, because Saul broke Israel's sworn oath and tried to destroy the Gibeonites.
options.note: Verse 1 traces the famine to Saul's violation of the covenant with the Gibeonites.

options.text: David's own sin with Bathsheba, which still lay unpunished.
options.note: The famine is traced to Saul's bloodguilt, not David's earlier sin.

options.text: Saul's mistreatment of the Gibeonites in violation of a covenant.
options.note: True, but this option omits that this incurred bloodguilt requiring atonement, the specific point the LORD makes.

options.text: The people's failure to keep the Passover.
options.note: The text names Saul's broken oath and bloodshed, not a failure to keep the Passover.

quiz.q: Why does David spare Mephibosheth when seven of Saul's descendants are handed over?

options.text: Because of his covenant oath before the LORD with Jonathan, Mephibosheth's father.
options.note: Verse 7 grounds the sparing of Mephibosheth in David's covenant with Jonathan.

options.text: Because Mephibosheth had helped David during Absalom's revolt.
options.note: The reason given is David's covenant with Jonathan, not Mephibosheth's actions in the revolt.

options.text: Because of his sworn covenant with Jonathan.
options.note: True, but this option omits that the oath was made specifically before the LORD, underscoring its binding weight.

options.text: Because Mephibosheth was too lame to pose any threat.
options.note: David spares him for the covenant's sake, not because of his disability.

quiz.q: What does Rizpah do, and how does David respond?

options.text: She keeps a long vigil guarding the exposed bodies from birds and beasts, moving David to bury Saul's house honorably.
options.note: Verses 10-14 record Rizpah's devotion and David's honorable burial in response.

options.text: She demands vengeance on the Gibeonites for killing her sons.
options.note: Rizpah keeps a mourning vigil; she does not demand vengeance.

options.text: She protects the bodies of the dead until David gives them burial.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking length of her vigil, from harvest until the rains, and its effect on David.

options.text: She flees the region in grief and is never heard from again.
options.note: The opposite; Rizpah stays and faithfully guards the bodies, prompting David's action.

quiz.q: What is the theological heart of David's dealing with the Gibeonites?

options.text: The need to make atonement for bloodguilt so that wrath is turned from the land and the LORD's inheritance is blessed.
options.note: Verse 3 shows David seeking atonement for the guilt, the key theme of the passage.

options.text: The need to reward the Gibeonites with silver and gold for their loss.
options.note: The Gibeonites explicitly refuse silver and gold; the issue is atonement for bloodguilt.

options.text: The need to cover the guilt Saul had incurred so the land could be healed.
options.note: True, but this option omits that this atonement points beyond itself to the true atonement in Christ.

options.text: The need to punish David's own household for its sins.
options.note: The guilt in view belongs to Saul's house, not David's; the theme is atonement for Saul's bloodguilt.

quiz.q: How does the chapter's atonement point to Christ?

options.text: Its costly, imperfect atonement for guilt foreshadows Christ, the innocent One who dies to cover the guilt of all and lift the curse.
options.note: The need for atonement points to the cross, Galatians 3:13 and Hebrews 9:22.

options.text: It teaches that God requires the execution of the innocent in every generation.
options.note: The chapter is a troubling ancient resolution, not a timeless requirement; Christ is the true and final atonement.

options.text: It shows that guilt requires atonement, fulfilled perfectly in Christ's sacrifice.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking contrast that Christ, unlike the seven, is the willing innocent One dying for the guilty.

options.text: It proves that broken covenants can simply be forgotten over time.
options.note: The opposite; the chapter shows broken covenants incur guilt that must be atoned for, not forgotten.

questions: God took a broken oath seriously even generations later. How seriously do you treat the promises and commitments you have made, especially those made before God?
David spared Mephibosheth for the sake of a covenant. How does it comfort you that God shows you mercy for the sake of Christ and His covenant, not because of your own record?
Rizpah kept a long, lonely vigil of faithful love over the dead. Where is God calling you to a costly, unseen faithfulness that honors those the world would forget?
The chapter aches for atonement to lift the curse from the land. How does knowing that Christ has made the final atonement change the way you carry your own guilt?

David's mighty men finished the fight against the giants when David himself grew weary. How can you take encouragement that God's work goes on through the whole community of His people, not one hero alone?

translationNotes.note: The BSB reads 'Merab' following some manuscripts and 1 Samuel 18:19, since Merab, not Michal, married Adriel; most Hebrew manuscripts read 'Michal,' likely a copyist's substitution of the better-known sister.

The BSB reads that Elhanan killed 'the brother of Goliath,' following 1 Chronicles 20:5; the Hebrew here lacks 'the brother of,' and the harmonized reading preserves that David, not Elhanan, killed Goliath himself in 1 Samuel 17.

The BSB footnote notes that 'a descendant of Rapha' can be rendered 'the giant'; the Rephaim were a people of great stature, and Goliath had been one such Philistine champion, so these battles complete the Goliath narrative.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 21:1-22. An em dash in the source at verse 20 was converted to a comma; no dashes remain in this file. The execution of Saul's descendants is handled as description within its ancient covenantal setting, not prescription.
